title: Alex Goes Off The Wagon

jokes.text: Finding a missing cabbie lies somewhere below 'There's a cat in heat disturbing the neighborhood.'
jokes.setup: Police have their priorities for what they'll investigate
jokes.punchline: Missing cabbie ranks below a cat in heat complaint
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational humor about police priorities with good economy and structure

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: Missing cabbie ranks below a cat in heat complaint

jokes.text: Tony breaking down the wrong apartment door first
jokes.setup: Tony went to check Alex's apartment
jokes.punchline: He broke down the wrong door first, then had to break down Alex's door too
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy that fits Tony's character perfectly - well-meaning but not the sharpest

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I went to check Alex's apartment.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I broke down the wrong door first.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Then I had to break down Alex's door too.

jokes.text: You're lucky the elevator opens by itself.
jokes.setup: Tony broke down two doors
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic observation about his door-breaking spree
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan follow-up that extends the door-breaking gag

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Tony, you broke down two doors? What are you, a battering ram?

dialogue.speaker: Tony Banta
dialogue.line: Hey, I was just trying to help!

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Help? The only thing you're helping is the door manufacturer's profit margins.

jokes.text: Tony asking people to stand behind each other because his neck hurts
jokes.setup: Tony injured his neck breaking down doors
jokes.punchline: He can't turn his head so needs people to line up
jokes.explanation: Absurd physical consequence of the door-breaking that creates ongoing visual comedy

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I can't turn my head. You're all gonna have to line up single file so I can see you.

dialogue.speaker: Others
dialogue.line: Why?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Because I can't turn my head!

jokes.text: You think maybe you could stand one behind the other? Oh, sure. Oh, thanks.
jokes.setup: Tony can't move his head due to injury
jokes.punchline: Others accommodating his neck problem by repositioning
jokes.explanation: Solid physical comedy callback to Tony's injury but not particularly innovative

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I can't move my head.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Don't worry, we'll work around it.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, we'll just stand where you can see us.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Thanks, guys.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No problem. We'll just rearrange ourselves.

jokes.text: Alex must be... Dead. No, don't say it... no.
jokes.setup: Louie's worry has convinced everyone Alex is in serious trouble
jokes.punchline: The dramatic pause and collective dread over stating the obvious
jokes.explanation: Extends the escalation of worry but feels somewhat predictable

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alex is dead.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What?!

dialogue.speaker: Marty
dialogue.line: No!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He can't be!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Well, he's not answering his phone.

jokes.text: Nardo, it looks like Reiger's gone to the harp farm.
jokes.setup: Everyone thinks Alex is dead
jokes.punchline: Louie's euphemism 'harp farm' for heaven/death
jokes.explanation: Clever euphemism that's both dark and character-appropriate for Louie's crude sensibility

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alex went to the harp farm.

jokes.text: Oh, great, Reiger, you're alive! Thank God!
jokes.setup: Louie was just expressing deep emotion about losing Alex
jokes.punchline: His sarcastic relief reveals his true callous nature
jokes.explanation: Perfect character consistency - Louie immediately returns to his abrasive self

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alex is gone? Thank God. Now I can finally get some work done around here without all his moral preaching and do-gooding slowing me down.

jokes.text: Oh, good, Nardo, good. Oh, good. Oh, take care of me, Nardo. Oh, good. Oh... Yeah, yeah, Nardo. Yeah, yeah... Take care of me, Nardo. Yeah, Nardo...
jokes.setup: Louie accepting Elaine's comfort
jokes.punchline: Getting increasingly creepy and inappropriate with his acceptance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie character moment turning touching into creepy, though repetitive

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I appreciate the sentiment, Elaine. You're a good egg.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's sweet, Louie. We're here for you.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know, you've always been nice to me. Real nice. I notice things like that.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, that's what friends do.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Friends. Yeah. You smell different when you're this close. Is that perfume or just... you?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Um, Louie...

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: No, no, it's nice. Real nice. You know, we should spend more time together. Alone. In the dark. We could just... sit there.

jokes.text: I had a found 200 bucks, if you know what I'm talking about.
jokes.setup: Alex is rationalizing why he gambled despite swearing it off
jokes.punchline: His transparent attempt to reframe the tip money as 'found money'
jokes.explanation: Believable addict rationalization that shows Alex's self-deception in real time

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: It's not gambling, it's found money. I found it in my pocket.

jokes.text: What are you made of, what're you made of stone?
jokes.setup: Alex walked away from a hot streak
jokes.punchline: Louie's incredulous reaction treats it like inhuman behavior
jokes.explanation: Louie's exasperation captures the gambler's mentality perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You walked away from a hot streak? What are you, crazy? Nobody walks away from a hot streak! You stay there until the fever breaks or you run out of money, whichever comes first!

jokes.text: Reiger, I know a table not five minutes away from here.
jokes.setup: Alex just explained his willpower in walking away
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately tries to get him to gamble again
jokes.explanation: Perfectly in character for Louie to be the devil on Alex's shoulder

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You got willpower? I got a guy who owes me two grand. You lend me fifty bucks, I'll knock him around, he'll pay up, you'll get your fifty back plus interest. Whaddya say?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: No, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're a tough guy. I respect that. But I also got twenty-seven dollars riding on the Knicks game tonight. You put in fifty, we split the winnings.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: My mother's sick. She needs an operation.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Your mother's not sick.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: She's gonna be if you don't lend me fifty bucks.

jokes.text: Think of how proud you'd be to walk away a second time.
jokes.setup: Alex is proud of walking away once
jokes.punchline: Louie's twisted logic that he could be twice as proud
jokes.explanation: Brilliant manipulation disguised as encouragement - perfectly captures enabling behavior with twisted logic

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I walked away from a guy once. I felt pretty good about myself.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well, I walked away from two guys once.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: So I was twice as proud.

jokes.text: Seven come eleven. Baby needs a new pair of shoes. Come on. Hey, little Joe, ho-ho! Come on, fever, fever.
jokes.setup: Louie is trying to tempt Alex back to gambling
jokes.punchline: Rapid-fire stream of gambling clichés to trigger Alex's addiction
jokes.explanation: Effective escalation of temptation using familiar gambling phrases

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Come on, Alex. Just one little bet. Remember the rush? The excitement? All that money changing hands? The bright lights, the ringing bells, the smell of a fresh deck of cards?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: No, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Red or black? You've got a feeling. I can see it in your eyes. You're thinking about it right now. Snake eyes, boxcars, seven come eleven. Double or nothing. The house is hot tonight, Alex. Your lucky number's coming up.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I said no.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: One hand. One little hand. Winner takes all. All or nothing. You're one roll away from a fortune, Alex. Can you hear those coins falling? That beautiful sound? Just one more time.

jokes.text: It's a good thing you got rid of her. She's a bad influence.
jokes.setup: Elaine was trying to stop Alex from gambling
jokes.punchline: Louie frames the voice of reason as a 'bad influence'
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic reversal - the enabler calling the voice of reason a bad influence

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Hey, hey, hey! What are you doing listening to this guy? He's a bad influence on you!

jokes.text: Sometimes I think they take advantage of me.
jokes.setup: Tony brought a cop down for nothing after breaking down doors
jokes.punchline: His mild complaint about being 'taken advantage of' after his over-the-top actions
jokes.explanation: Nice callback that shows Tony's oblivious nature

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I get taken advantage of. I break down doors, I chase people, I risk my life, and for what? Nothing!

jokes.text: Hey, this beats stealing computer secrets, don't it?
jokes.setup: Gambling and winning money
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to espionage as previous occupation
jokes.explanation: Unexpected dark background detail delivered casually for good effect

dialogue.speaker: Unknown character
dialogue.line: I won big at the track today!

dialogue.speaker: Unknown character
dialogue.line: Yeah, I used to be a spy, you know. I can read people.

jokes.text: The fat lady wants a hard eight. / Don't apologize for him, just roll a hard eight, big nose.
jokes.setup: Dealer calls player 'fat lady'
jokes.punchline: Player responds with 'big nose' insult
jokes.explanation: Basic tit-for-tat insult exchange without much cleverness

dialogue.speaker: Dealer
dialogue.line: Hey, fat lady!

dialogue.speaker: Player
dialogue.line: Big nose.

jokes.text: Reiger, I'm not ashamed to say I love you.
jokes.setup: Alex is on a winning streak
jokes.punchline: Louie's over-the-top emotional declaration
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing how winning money affects Louie's usually callous demeanor

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alex, you're on a winning streak! You're the hottest cab driver in this city! I'm so proud of you, I could cry! In fact, I am crying!

jokes.text: That's what separates the men from the mutants, Louie.
jokes.setup: Alex is about to make a desperate bet
jokes.punchline: His macho declaration using 'mutants' instead of 'boys'
jokes.explanation: Decent variation on common phrase that fits Alex's desperation

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'm gonna bet everything I've got. Let the mutants think what they want!

jokes.text: I'd rather go home and lay all this money on my bed and get naked and roll around in it until it sticks to my sweaty body.
jokes.setup: Louie says he feels bad for Alex losing
jokes.punchline: His grotesque description of what he'd rather do with his winnings
jokes.explanation: Memorably disgusting image that perfectly captures Louie's crude, selfish nature and creates strong visual comedy

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I feel bad for Alex losing. You know what I'd do with the money? I'd take it and stuff it in a big pile, and then I'd dive into it like Scrooge McDuck. Then I'd take what's left and buy a bunch of parrots, teach 'em to say dirty words, and let 'em loose in church. Then with whatever's left after that, I'd hire a guy to follow me around all day and slap me every time I say something stupid.

jokes.text: I gambled a little while; I, uh, had a few laughs, and I came out about even.
jokes.setup: Alex lost everything and is now at another gambling place
jokes.punchline: His massive understatement of the situation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Alex's continued self-deception and minimization

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, I've had a little streak of bad luck.

jokes.text: Oh, they're, they're watching a rerun of All in the Family here.
jokes.setup: Elaine hears casino noise in the background
jokes.punchline: Alex's absurd excuse for the gambling sounds
jokes.explanation: Believable desperate lie that shows how far Alex will go to hide his gambling

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What is that noise in the background?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Oh, that? That's just the guys playing pinball in the garage.

jokes.text: I'll remember it because they're all ages that Frank Sinatra has been.
jokes.setup: Jim needs to remember the combination 3-14-60
jokes.punchline: His bizarre mnemonic device involving Frank Sinatra's ages
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd Jim Ignatowski logic - seemingly random but internally consistent with his character's unique mental processes

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: 3-14-60... okay, let me think of a way to remember this. Frank Sinatra was 3 years old when he was born, 14 when he was a teenager, and 60 when he was 60.

jokes.text: Accidents will happen.
jokes.setup: Jim keeps saying this phrase throughout the phone call
jokes.punchline: His non-sequitur response that becomes a recurring element
jokes.explanation: Good running gag that captures Jim's disconnected thought patterns

dialogue.line: I need more context about the specific joke to accurately parse the dialogue. Please provide the actual SETUP and PUNCHLINE text with the complete dialogue, as I cannot reliably reconstruct speaker attribution from placeholder descriptions alone.

jokes.text: What, no exchange of pleasantries? Hello, Jim, how you doing? / Hello, Alex. Quite well, thank you. Care for a cracker?
jokes.setup: Alex is desperate and demanding his money
jokes.punchline: Jim insists on polite social interaction and offers crackers
jokes.explanation: Excellent character contrast - Jim's oblivious politeness against Alex's desperation creates perfect comedic tension

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Jim, I need my money now. This is serious.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Alex, we're civilized people. Let's sit down and talk about this properly.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I don't want to sit down! I want my money!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, the least you can do is have some crackers while we discuss it.

jokes.text: I took the precaution of putting it here so I wouldn't eat your money.
jokes.setup: Alex can't find the envelope in the cracker box
jokes.punchline: Jim's concern about accidentally eating money
jokes.explanation: Pure Jim logic - the idea that he might accidentally eat an envelope full of money is both absurd and somehow believable for his character

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You're not worried about finding the envelope?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm worried about accidentally eating it. I love those crackers.

jokes.text: Well, you were only off by one. / How much do you get for that? Nothing! I'm broke. / Well, then that was pretty dumb of you to bet all of your money like that.
jokes.setup: Alex bet everything on 11, rolled 12
jokes.punchline: Jim's matter-of-fact assessment of Alex's poor decision
jokes.explanation: Jim's blunt honesty provides perfect deadpan commentary on Alex's gambling addiction

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, you missed it by one.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I know that, Jim.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, but you bet everything you had. So now you got nothing. That's what I call missing it by one.

jokes.text: Well, you were only off by one. How much do you get... How much do you get for that? Nothing! I'm broke.
jokes.setup: Alex betting on 11 and rolling 12
jokes.punchline: Jim's cheerful consolation followed by Alex's reality
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Jim's optimism and gambling reality

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Hey, you were close! You got an 11 on your next roll.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Jim, I just rolled a 12. There is no next roll.

jokes.text: Well, then that was pretty dumb of you to bet all of your money like that.
jokes.setup: Alex explaining he's broke after betting everything
jokes.punchline: Jim's matter-of-fact assessment of the obvious
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim bluntness stating the obvious with innocent delivery

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You're broke.

jokes.text: Well, the important thing is we had fun. And we were polite.
jokes.setup: Alex has lost everything
jokes.punchline: Jim's positive spin focusing on manners over money
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim moment - completely missing the gravity of the situation and focusing on social niceties

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know, Alex, you've lost everything, but look on the bright side — at least you still have your manners. You're broke, but you're polite about it.

jokes.text: Poor, lost souls? Wretched refuse on the dung heap of human greed?
jokes.setup: Jim warns Alex about ending up like other gamblers
jokes.punchline: His over-the-top poetic description of gambling addicts
jokes.explanation: Jim's florid language creates a funny contrast with the seedy setting

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You end up like the others. You start out thinking you can beat the odds, but then the fever takes hold. Your eyes get this wild, desperate look. You sell everything—your car, your clothes, your dignity. You're down to your last nickel, standing outside a casino at three in the morning in the pouring rain, your shoes held together with tape, talking to yourself about the system you've discovered that's gonna make it all back. And nobody recognizes you anymore because you've become this hollow shell, this ghost of a man, muttering about hot streaks and lucky numbers and how close you came, always how close you came...

jokes.text: You wouldn't know it to look at me now, but I wasn't always this together.
jokes.setup: Jim is about to tell a serious story about his past
jokes.punchline: His belief that he's currently 'together' when he's clearly still very scattered
jokes.explanation: Excellent character irony - Jim's complete lack of self-awareness about his current state

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know, I've really got my life together now. I mean, I used to be a mess—lost, confused, didn't know what I wanted. But those days are behind me.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Now I wake up every morning with a clear sense of purpose and direction.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I know exactly what I'm doing with my life... mostly. Well, I think I do. I mean, I'm pretty sure I locked my apartment door this morning. Or was that yesterday?

jokes.text: No walls, just sand as far as the eye could see.
jokes.setup: Jim describes a place like the gambling den but different
jokes.punchline: He's describing the desert as if it's an indoor location
jokes.explanation: Jim's drug-addled storytelling creates a surreal narrative that becomes increasingly absurd

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It's like a gambling den, but without the walls, without the ceiling, without the floor... just sand as far as the eye can see.

jokes.text: We were down to our last mushroom and both of us wanted it.
jokes.setup: Jim and an old shaman were doing psychedelic mushrooms
jokes.punchline: Setting up a moral dilemma over the last drug
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the moral lesson, though the humor comes more from the absurd situation

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: There's only one mushroom left. Who should take it?

dialogue.speaker: Shaman
dialogue.line: You must decide, my friend. It is a test of character.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, you're older. You should take it.

dialogue.speaker: Shaman
dialogue.line: No, no. You are younger. You have more years to enjoy the visions.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I insist. Take it. You're a shaman. You probably know what you're doing.

dialogue.speaker: Shaman
dialogue.line: And you are a taxi driver. You probably need it more.

jokes.text: I looked up to the sky and I said, 'Look at that star.' And he looked. Who wouldn't?
jokes.setup: Jim planned to distract the shaman
jokes.punchline: His innocent aside about the universal appeal of star-gazing
jokes.explanation: Jim's innocent commentary during a story about deception adds layers to his character

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know, it's funny... no matter what culture you're from, everyone looks up at the stars and wonders about the universe.

jokes.text: And it wasn't just because he jumped off the cliff trying to fly.
jokes.setup: Jim felt better about giving the shaman the mushroom
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation that the shaman died as a result
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark punchline delivered with Jim's characteristic casual tone - the horror is in the matter-of-fact delivery

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, I felt a lot better about giving that shaman the mushroom.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Turns out he died.

jokes.text: Welcome to rock bottom.
jokes.setup: Jim describes Alex's current pathetic situation
jokes.punchline: His formal welcome to rock bottom like it's a destination
jokes.explanation: Good delivery of the rock bottom concept with Jim's formal politeness

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Welcome to rock bottom. We have a gift shop.

jokes.text: Hey, Jim, look at that light.
jokes.setup: Alex uses Jim's own distraction technique against him
jokes.punchline: Perfect reversal using the star-gazing story
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that shows Alex learned from Jim's story and turns it against him - perfect structure and timing

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know, Jim, speaking of distractions, I was out last night looking at the stars. And I got to thinking - have you ever really looked at the stars? I mean really looked at them?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Alex, what are you—

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: No, wait, hear me out. The Milky Way alone contains an estimated 200 billion stars. 200 billion! Each one potentially with its own planetary system. And we're standing here on this tiny speck of dust, worried about our little problems.

jokes.text: What's the rush? How often do you get to see a light like that?
jokes.setup: Jim wants to leave after being tricked
jokes.punchline: Alex continues the star-gazing theme sarcastically
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to the distraction callback that extends the reversal

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm leaving. You tricked me.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Jim, wait. Look up at the stars. Isn't it beautiful? Makes you realize how small your problems really are.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, right now the only star I see is the one I'm gonna see when you hit me.

tmdb_overview: In this episode, we learn that Alex can be a compulsive gambler. Alex even scares Louie off with his compulsion, but Jim talks some sense into Alex at the end (after having bankrolled him initially).
insight: Alex's relapse spawns 40 jokes in 40 minutes—character work sustains the drama.
